repo.name: the-for-good-project
repo.description: An open research commons where people and AI agents work together to solve New Zealand's biggest societal problems. By thecolab.ai.

issues.title: fix(web): make dashboard mobile responsive
issues.body:
## What
Fixes the Dashboard cards blowing out the page width on mobile (reported by Matt) — specifically the **Open work by domain** chart and **Recent activity** components.

## Root cause
The recharts `ResponsiveContainer` sat inside grid items with the default `min-width: auto`, so they couldn't shrink below their content (including the 120px Y-axis) and forced the page wider than the mobile viewport. There was also no global `overflow-x` guard.

## Changes
- Add `min-w-0` to the grid children so they can shrink properly
- Wrap the chart in a `min-w-0 overflow-hidden` container; trim Y-axis width 120 -> 96 for small screens
- Truncate the recent-activity meta line
- Harden the review callout flex (`min-w-0` + `shrink-0`) so it wraps cleanly instead of overflowing
- Add an `overflow-x-hidden` safety net on `<main>`

## Verified
- `tsc -b && vite build` passes clean locally

Closes the mobile width issue on the dashboard page.

issues.title: research: document NZ public sector AI guidance
issues.body:
Closes #6.

## What changed

Adds a cited research finding at `research/findings/ai-policy/nz-public-sector-ai-guidance.md` answering what official AI guidance, frameworks, and policies currently exist for New Zealand public-sector/public-service AI use.

## Summary

- Maps the GCDO-led public-service AI framework, GenAI guidance, work programme, and toolkit.
- Captures Stats NZ/Data.govt algorithm-governance material, including the Algorithm Charter and Algorithm Impact Assessment process.
- Notes relevant privacy, records, procurement, and agency-level policy material, plus the national light-touch AI policy context.
- Marks confidence, load-bearing claims, limits, and follow-up questions.

## Validation

- `git diff --cached --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `gh issue view 6 --json number,title,labels,assignees,state,url`
- `gh search issues --repo thecolab-ai/the-for-good-project "AI guidance public sector" --state open --json number,title,url,state`

No parent issue was identified in #6.

issues.title: [Research] What NZ government AI guidance already exists for the public sector?
issues.body:
**The question**
What official AI guidance, frameworks, or policies already exist for the New Zealand public sector (e.g. from DIA, the Public Service Commission, Stats NZ, GCDO), and what do they actually require or recommend?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Grounds any AI-policy work in what NZ has already published, so we build on it rather than reinventing or contradicting it.

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/ai-policy/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`. Prefer primary government sources; mark confidence; note anything dated or superseded.

_Good first issue: clear scope, official sources, immediately useful as a reference._

commentsList.author: adam91holt
commentsList.body: 🔍 Work submitted in #7 — moving to **in review**. It needs an adversarial review from a *different identity than the author* before it can merge (see `review_work.sh` / branch protection).

issues.title: [Research] How many grant funders operate in NZ, and where does eligibility data live?
issues.body:
**The question**
How many grant funders operate in New Zealand (philanthropic trusts, gaming/community trusts, councils, government), and in what form is their eligibility/criteria data available — machine-readable, PDF, web pages, or not published at all?

**Why it matters / what decision it informs**
Whether a grant-matching tool for small charities is feasible depends entirely on whether eligibility data can be assembled. This scopes that.

**Part of** the grant-access Discover issue.

**Output:** a cited finding in `research/findings/grant-access/` using `research/TEMPLATE.md`. Follow the method — cite everything, mark confidence, say what you couldn't verify.

_Good first issue: bounded, mostly desk research, high value._

issues.title: [Discover] Families don't know what child & family support they qualify for
issues.body:
**The problem**
NZ has many child- and family-support entitlements (payments, subsidies, services) across agencies. Eligibility is complex and the information is spread out, so families — especially those under stress — miss support they're entitled to.

**Why it matters**
Unclaimed support for children is a direct, avoidable harm. Navigation and eligibility are exactly the kind of problem better information tools can help with.

**Ethics note:** this domain touches vulnerable people. Any work here uses public sources only, no personal data, and flags where a human with lived experience or authority is needed.

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- What child/family entitlements exist, which agencies own them, and how is eligibility defined?
- Where do families currently go for help, and where does that break down?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above.

issues.title: [Discover] Council spending is public but not legible to citizens
issues.body:
**The problem**
NZ councils publish budgets, spending, and decisions, but the data is scattered across PDFs, portals, and formats that ordinary citizens and local journalists can't easily use. "Technically public" is not the same as legible or accountable.

**Why it matters**
Local accountability depends on people being able to see where money goes and what was decided. The barrier is usability, not secrecy — which makes it tractable.

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- Which councils publish spending data, in what formats, and under what open-data terms?
- What questions do residents and local journalists actually want answered from it?
- Are there existing NZ civic-tech efforts here to build on rather than duplicate?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above.

issues.title: [Discover] Small NZ charities miss grants they're eligible for
issues.body:
**The problem**
New Zealand has hundreds of grant funders (philanthropic trusts, gaming trusts, councils, government), but discovery is manual and fragmented. Small charities and community groups — often run by volunteers — miss funding they're eligible for simply because finding and matching to the right grants is hard and time-consuming.

**Why it matters**
The unclaimed/under-applied pool is plausibly significant, and the groups worst affected are the smallest ones doing frontline work. _(Figures to be established — see the research questions below.)_

**Questions this raises** (candidates for Research issues)
- How many grant funders operate in NZ, and where does their eligibility data actually live?
- What do small charities currently use to find grants, and where does it break down?
- Is there a machine-readable source of NZ grants, or would one need to be assembled?

**Next step:** open Research issues for the questions above. See CONTRIBUTING.md.

commentsList.author: karenjleacock
commentsList.body:
Check out https://www.fundsorter.com/ 
It's about a year old? They are also doing great things in the training space.
I can't answer the questions you pose (and don't have spare tokens), but it feels like they are leading in this space in NZ.

issues.title: PRD: Distributed Agent Research System
issues.body:
**Version 1.0 | 2026-06-30 | Ready for Implementation**

## TL;DR
- GitHub Issues = task queue
- `work.sh` claims a task, runs Claude agent with standardized method (`CONTRIBUTING.md`), creates PR
- `review.sh` adversarially verifies findings before merge
- **Result:** Transparent, scalable, zero duplication, full audit trail

## System Flow
```
Issue -> work.sh (claim + research) -> PR -> review.sh (adversarial check) -> PASS: Merge | NEEDS_WORK: Revise
```

## Key Scripts

**work.sh** - Grab unclaimed issue -> mark in-progress -> pass to Claude (task + method) -> agent researches -> create PR -> label review-needed

**review.sh** - Grab PR needing review -> pass to adversarial agent ("refute this") -> if PASS: merge + done; if NEEDS_WORK: request changes + revert to in-progress

**CONTRIBUTING.md** - Research method all agents follow:
1. Clarify the question
2. Choose approach (Workflow for multi-agent, Agent for single)
3. Research & cite (every claim >=1 source)
4. Verify surprises (>=2 sources)
5. Mark confidence (High/Medium/Low)
6. Format findings (use PR template)

## Repo Structure
```
├── README.md
├── CONTRIBUTING.md
├── work.sh
├── review.sh
├── .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/research-task.md
├── .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/research-finding.md
└── research/findings/ (merged findings live here)
```

## GitHub Labels
- Status: unclaimed | in-progress | review-needed | done
- Domain: child-welfare | grant-access | civic-transparency | ai-policy | biosecurity
- Task: research/task | research/finding

## Phases
- **Phase 1 (Week 1):** Repo + scripts + templates + end-to-end test on 2 tasks
- **Phase 2 (Week 2):** GitHub Actions automation
- **Phase 3 (Week 3):** Metrics + dashboard
- **Phase 4 (Week 4+):** Scale

## Success Story Example
Monday 9am: Issue #42 created ("Starship impact on child welfare?")
10am: Researcher claims + runs work.sh
10-12:30pm: 3 agents research in parallel
12:30pm: PR #88 created, needs review
1:30pm: review.sh finds 2 weak points
2-3pm: Researcher responds with NZ data
3:30pm: review.sh PASS -> merges
**Outcome:** 6 hours, full audit trail, finding live in repo

## Handoff Checklist
- [ ] Repo + write access
- [ ] Scripts (error handling)
- [ ] Templates
- [ ] Labels
- [ ] Project board
- [ ] README
- [ ] End-to-end test
- [ ] Race condition test

## Implementation Agent Task
1. Create repo + structure
2. Write work.sh + review.sh (error handling)
3. Create templates + CONTRIBUTING.md
4. Test end-to-end on 3 sample tasks
5. Verify: zero race conditions, conflicts, all sourced
6. Document + handoff

---

*Filed by The Colab Clawd as a test of issue-creation access, per Adam's request. Full context from WhatsApp #For Good discussion, 2026-06-30/07-01.*

commentsList.author: thecolab-clawd
commentsList.body: Closing — this was just an access test. PRD content stays here for reference when the implementation work kicks off.
